name: tool-interface-analysis
description: Analyze tool registration, schema generation, and error feedback mechanisms in agent frameworks. Use when (1) understanding how tools are defined and registered, (2) evaluating schema generation approaches (introspection vs manual), (3) tracing error feedback loops to the LLM, (4) assessing retry and self-correction mechanisms, or (5) comparing tool interfaces across frameworks.
qualityReason: Deep technical analysis of tool interfaces
capabilities: Analyzes tool modeling patterns including ABC, Protocol, Decorator, and Pydantic approaches
Documents schema generation methods from introspection, Pydantic, decorator-based, and manual definitions
Maps tool registration patterns including declarative lists, registry, discovery, and factory patterns
Evaluates error feedback mechanisms and retry strategies for LLM self-correction
Catalogs built-in tool categories and implementations across frameworks
Produces structured framework comparison matrices and analysis documents
author: Dowwie
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing no executable code. It provides analysis patterns and templates for studying tool interfaces in AI agent frameworks. All 162 static findings are false positives: the scanner misidentified markdown code block delimitators as Ruby backticks, placeholder documentation examples as credentials, and metadata URL fields as hardcoded URLs. No actual code execution, credential access, or network operations exist in this skill.
valueStatement: Understanding tool interfaces is critical for AI agent framework design. This skill analyzes how frameworks model, register, and execute tools with proper error handling. Use it to evaluate schema generation approaches, trace error feedback loops, and compare tool interfaces across frameworks.
limitations: Requires existing codebase to analyze - no standalone execution
Analysis quality depends on code readability and existing documentation
May need manual verification for complex framework interactions
Limited to patterns visible in source code, not runtime behavior

useCases.title: Design Tool Abstraction Layer
useCases.description: Analyze existing frameworks to design optimal tool interfaces with proper schema generation and error handling patterns

useCases.title: Compare Agent Frameworks
useCases.description: Evaluate different approaches to tool modeling, registration, and execution across popular AI agent frameworks

useCases.title: Audit Tool Execution Patterns
useCases.description: Examine error handling, validation, and sandboxing mechanisms in agent tool execution

promptTemplates.title: Basic Tool Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze the tool interface patterns in this framework. Focus on how tools are defined, registered, and executed. Include schema generation methods and error handling approaches.

promptTemplates.title: Schema Generation Deep Dive
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare the schema generation methods used in this framework versus others. Document the trade-offs between introspection, Pydantic, decorator-based, and manual approaches.

promptTemplates.title: Error Handling Audit
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze the error feedback mechanisms in this framework. How are errors reported back to the LLM? What retry and self-correction mechanisms exist?

promptTemplates.title: Framework Comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare tool interfaces across multiple frameworks. Create a matrix showing modeling patterns, schema generation, registration methods, and error handling strategies.

bestPractices: Use structured error feedback with actionable suggestions that enable LLM self-correction
Implement schema validation before tool execution to prevent runtime errors and provide clear error messages
Design tool abstractions that support both static registration and dynamic discovery for flexibility
antiPatterns: Silent error handling that prevents the LLM from understanding why tool execution failed
Manual schema definitions that can drift from implementation and cause validation issues
Tight coupling between tools and specific LLM providers that limits portability

faq.question: Which frameworks are compatible with this analysis skill?
faq.answer: Works with any Python-based agent framework including LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Pydantic-AI, OpenAI Agents SDK, and custom implementations.

faq.question: What file patterns should I analyze for tool interfaces?
faq.answer: Focus on files matching **/tool*.py, **/schema*.py, **/registry*.py, **/executor*.py, and **/builtin*.py patterns.

faq.question: How does this skill integrate with other analysis skills?
faq.answer: Use after codebase-mapping to identify relevant files. Results feed into comparative-matrix and architecture-synthesis skills.

faq.question: Is the analysis automated or manual?
faq.answer: Provides structured analysis patterns and templates. You apply them to the codebase and document findings following the provided format.

faq.question: What if the framework uses non-standard tool patterns?
faq.answer: Document any unique patterns found. The templates are flexible and can accommodate custom implementations beyond common patterns.

faq.question: How detailed should the analysis document be?
faq.answer: Focus on the core tool abstraction, schema generation method, registration pattern, and error handling. Include code examples and file references.
